skill.name: cocoindex
skill.description: Guides users through building and operating CocoIndex data transformation flows for AI indexing, search, extraction, and knowledge graph pipelines.
skill.summary: Build CocoIndex flows for AI data indexing, search, extraction, and pipeline operations.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows most are Markdown command examples, CocoIndex API method names, or placeholder configuration snippets. The skill has legitimate elevated risk because it teaches users to run CLI operations, handle .env secrets, connect to databases, and call external APIs. No prompt-injection attempt, malicious endpoint, credential exfiltration, or confirmed destructive intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Environment and secret handling guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE as an operational risk: the skill instructs users to check environment variables, create .env files, and configure database and LLM API keys. This is legitimate CocoIndex setup guidance, but users could expose secrets if they paste real keys into prompts, commit .env files, or display database URLs in terminal output. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: Multiple inspected locations show direct .env, database URL, and API-key guidance, but values are placeholders and there is no exfiltration behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple inspected locations show direct .env, database URL, and API-key guidance. Values are placeholders and no exfiltration behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: External API and network integration examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE as a contextual risk: the skill documents outbound requests and authenticated API patterns for enrichment and LLM workflows. This is expected for data enrichment pipelines, but users should verify destinations, avoid untrusted endpoints, and avoid embedding raw secrets in generated code. Confidence: 0.76. Confidence reasoning: The HTTP and API-key pattern is explicit, but the example is generic documentation rather than a hidden network call by the skill itself.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The HTTP and API-key pattern is explicit in documentation. The example is generic and not a hidden network call by the skill itself.

medium_findings.title: Destructive flow and target-management commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE as a data-loss risk: the CLI reference documents drop and recreate operations that can remove internal storage and target resources. The file labels the action as destructive, so this is legitimate documentation rather than malicious intent. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The destructive behavior is directly described, but the warning text reduces ambiguity and indicates intended administrative use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destructive behavior is directly described in the CLI reference. The surrounding warning indicates legitimate administrative documentation.

low_findings.title: Dynamic eval static findings are CocoIndex API calls
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for dynamic code execution: the detected eval references call CocoIndex transform-flow evaluation methods for query embeddings. They are not Python eval() calls and do not execute arbitrary source text in the inspected examples. Confidence: 0.93. Confidence reasoning: Each inspected line uses text_to_embedding.eval(...) as a library API method for embedding search queries, with no evidence of arbitrary code execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each inspected line uses text_to_embedding.eval(...) as a library API method. No arbitrary code execution pattern was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown backticks were counted as shell execution
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for most external-command hits: many detections are inline package names, CLI command references, or fenced examples in documentation. They can lead users to run local CocoIndex commands, but the skill does not contain executable scripts or hidden command execution. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: Representative locations show Markdown command formatting and normal CLI documentation rather than Ruby backtick execution or subprocess calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative locations show Markdown command formatting and normal CLI documentation. No Ruby backtick execution or subprocess call was found.

low_findings.title: Weak-cryptography hits are documentation false positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for confirmed weak cryptography: inspected static locations near the weak-crypto hits contain flow definitions, data exports, warnings, or generated field examples, not MD5, SHA1, custom encryption, or password hashing logic. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: Targeted review and keyword checks found no weak crypto routine in the audited files; the scanner appears to have matched unrelated terms or line context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review and keyword checks found no weak crypto routine. The scanner appears to have matched unrelated terms or context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secrets and database URLs in generated user workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks assistants to help users configure COCOINDEX_DATABASE_URL and provider API keys. This is expected for CocoIndex, but generated workflows should avoid logging full values, storing real keys in prompts, or committing .env files. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: Secret handling is explicit in setup guidance, but examples use placeholders and no malicious collection path was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Secret handling is explicit in setup guidance. Examples use placeholders and no malicious collection path was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound authenticated API calls in custom functions
dangerous_patterns.description: The custom-function reference shows requests.get with a bearer token header. This is a normal extension pattern, but user-generated code must validate endpoints and avoid sending sensitive identifiers to untrusted services. Confidence: 0.76. Confidence reasoning: The network request and bearer-token pattern are direct, but the endpoint is user-configured documentation rather than a hardcoded suspicious destination.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network request and bearer-token pattern are direct. The endpoint is user-configured rather than a hardcoded suspicious destination.

content.value_statement: AI indexing projects need reliable pipelines for documents, embeddings, extraction, and target stores. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through CocoIndex flow design, custom functions, CLI operations, and API workflows.
actual_capabilities: Designs CocoIndex flow definitions for local files, databases, cloud storage, and streaming-style updates.
Explains transformation patterns for chunking, embeddings, LLM extraction, and query handlers.
Guides custom CocoIndex function creation with standalone functions and spec plus executor classes.
Provides CLI workflows for setup, evaluate, update, live updates, show, and drop operations.
Shows API integration patterns for batch jobs, web applications, and live updater control.
Documents target exports for vector databases, relational stores, and graph databases.
limitations: Requires users to provide their own CocoIndex project context, database access, and provider credentials.
Does not validate real infrastructure permissions, network policies, or production data governance rules.
Examples may need adjustment for specific CocoIndex versions and target database schemas.
Generated commands can change or delete data if users run destructive CLI operations.

use_cases.title: Create document search indexes
use_cases.description: Build a CocoIndex flow that loads documents, chunks text, creates embeddings, and exports results to a vector database.

use_cases.title: Extract structured records
use_cases.description: Use LLM extraction patterns to turn raw files or database rows into structured records for analytics or automation.

use_cases.title: Operate indexing workflows
use_cases.description: Run setup, evaluate, update, live update, and inspection commands while managing environment configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a first flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new indexing project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a CocoIndex flow for documents in [source]. I want [search or extraction goal] and target storage in [database]. Ask for any missing details first.

prompt_templates.title: Add embeddings
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a CocoIndex pattern for chunking [file type] content, embedding each chunk with [model provider], and exporting vectors to [target]. Include setup and update steps.

prompt_templates.title: Build a custom function
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a CocoIndex custom function for [transformation]. It needs [configuration], should use caching if appropriate, and must return fields for [target schema].

prompt_templates.title: Review production operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my CocoIndex flow and CLI workflow for production readiness. Focus on credentials, destructive commands, live updates, concurrency limits, and rollback steps.

output_examples.input: Design a CocoIndex flow for PDF search with OpenAI embeddings and Postgres pgvector.
output_examples.output: A structured plan covering dependencies, environment variables, source configuration, chunking, embedding, collector fields, Postgres export, setup commands, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: I need to enrich product records with data from an external API.
output_examples.output: A custom-function approach that separates configuration from execution, caches expensive clients, validates errors, and warns against exposing API keys.

output_examples.input: My flow changed and the target schema no longer matches.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist that starts with non-destructive inspection, explains setup refresh options, and clearly flags drop commands as destructive.

best_practices: Use placeholder secrets in prompts and load real credentials from approved local or platform secret stores.
Run evaluate and show commands before update, live, or drop operations on important data.
Keep flow names, collector fields, target schemas, and primary keys explicit before generating code.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real API keys, database passwords, or cloud credentials into chat prompts.
Do not run drop or recreate commands against production targets without a backup and approval path.
Do not send enrichment requests to untrusted endpoints or mix sensitive source data with unknown APIs.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants design and operate CocoIndex data transformation flows for search, extraction, and indexing.

faq.question: Does it run CocoIndex commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users decide which commands to run in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it help with vector search?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for chunking content, generating embeddings, and exporting vectors to supported targets.

faq.question: Can it create custom transformation functions?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains standalone functions and spec plus executor classes for reusable CocoIndex operations.

faq.question: What credentials may be needed?
faq.answer: Typical projects need a CocoIndex database URL and optional LLM, cloud, or external API credentials.

faq.question: What safety steps should users follow?
faq.answer: Use placeholder secrets in prompts, review generated commands, and treat drop or schema recreation steps as destructive.
